fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to get the terminal log colorizer shipped for the browser-based job view. Basically right now when you look at build logs in the UI you either see a wall of garbage escape characters or everything is plain unformatted text, and users are complaining it's unreadable especially for error output from tools like lager or webpack that do heavy terminal styling. We need something that takes that raw console output and turns it into proper HTML so it looks right in the browser. There are a few tricky bits — some of our internal log framework embeds special metadata payloads inside concealed text regions (you know, similar to how we handled that marker injection thing in the annotation layer before), and those absolutely cannot get stripped out even though normal hidden text should be. Also we have a couple of named color themes that need to work differently — one of them should wrap the whole output in a container with default colors, and colors that aren't covered by a named theme should fall back to indexed or rgb values. The action marker insertion feature also needs to spit out a specific output format with a normalized placeholder for the action id. Ping me if anything is unclear but let's try to get the core rendering logic separated from the I/O stuff this time, we got burned last time by having everything tangled together.

trigger_keywords: console note marker preserved
special metadata payload not stripped
concealed text with ha: prefix kept
embedded annotation survives concealment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Concealed text segments (ESC[8m...ESC[0m) are normally suppressed entirely, but if the concealed payload starts with the prefix 'ha:' (e.g., '\u001b[8mha:CONSOLENOTE\u001b[0m'), it must be emitted verbatim — including the surrounding ESC[8m and ESC[0m escape sequences — and not stripped. This is the ConsoleNote marker passthrough behavior.

trigger_keywords: vga palette wraps output in div
default foreground background container
palette with defaults adds outer wrapper
which palettes add a wrapping div element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the selected palette defines both a default foreground and a default background color (i.e., 'default_foreground' and 'default_background' are non-null index values), the entire rendered HTML output must be wrapped in a '
...
' container. The 'vga' palette has default_foreground=7 (#AAAAAA) and default_background=0 (#000000), so it produces this wrapper. The 'xterm' palette has no defaults (both are 'none'), so it does not wrap. The 'css' palette also has no defaults.

trigger_keywords: inverse video color swap
ESC[7m foreground background invert
reverse video rendering behavior
what happens when no fg or bg is set and inverse is on
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When inverse video (ESC[7m) is active with no explicit foreground set, the foreground renders as '#FFFFFF' (white) and the background renders as 'currentColor'. When explicit fg/bg colors are set, inverse swaps them: the original fg becomes the background-color and the original bg becomes the color. When ESC[27m disables inverse, the colors revert to their pre-inversion logical values. A full reset (ESC[0m or ESC[m) clears both inverse mode and all colors.

trigger_keywords: faint attribute CSS
ESC[2m rendering
font-weight lighter span
how is dim or faint text rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The faint/dim attribute (SGR code 2) renders as '...'. It is distinct from bold (SGR 1 which renders as ''). When faint is combined with a foreground color, the faint span is nested inside the color span: 'text'.

trigger_keywords: character set escape filtered
ESC(0 sequence removed
designate character set stripped
what happens to ESC open-paren sequences
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Character-set designation sequences of the form ESC followed by '(' and a single character (e.g., '\u001b(0' for DEC line drawing) are treated as non-rendering control sequences and must be completely filtered from the output. Surrounding visible characters are preserved unchanged.

trigger_keywords: resolve color map default surface
which palette is default for build wrapper
surface name default palette resolution
what palette does log_action use when none requested
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The resolve_color_map operation returns the palette name in the format 'color_map=<name>\n'. When 'requested' is null or empty string, the default is always 'xterm' regardless of the surface name ('build_wrapper', 'pipeline_step', 'log_action', etc.). When a non-empty palette name is explicitly provided in 'requested', that name is returned as-is.

trigger_keywords: palette summary output format
palette_summary fields and order
what fields does palette definition expose
how are bright colors and defaults reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The palette_summary operation emits a newline-terminated key=value text block with this exact field order: name, normal_black, normal_red, normal_green, normal_yellow, normal_blue, normal_magenta, normal_cyan, normal_white, bright_black, bright_red, bright_green, bright_yellow, bright_blue, bright_magenta, bright_cyan, bright_white, default_foreground, default_background. When a palette has no defaults, default_foreground and default_background are reported as 'none'. For vga, default_foreground=7 and default_background=0 (the index values, not hex).

trigger_keywords: insert action marker format
action marker position output
data-ansicolor-action placeholder
what does the annotated text field look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The insert_action_marker operation outputs a single line: 'annotated_text=\n'. The marker is a void HTML element '
' inserted at the specified character position (0-based) in the original text. The action identifier inside the attribute is always the literal placeholder string '' regardless of the action parameter value ('start', 'stop', etc.) or palette.

trigger_keywords: independent foreground background reset
ESC[39m only resets foreground
ESC[49m only resets background
partial color reset without losing other styles
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SGR code 39 resets only the foreground color while leaving background color and all text attributes (bold, underline, etc.) active. SGR code 49 resets only the background color while leaving foreground color and text attributes active. This is distinct from SGR 0 which resets all attributes. The HTML nesting must be repaired accordingly — open color spans are closed and any still-active text-attribute tags are re-opened outside the closed color span.

trigger_keywords: xterm bright colors hex values
bright blue is steelblue not pure blue
which hex codes do bright ANSI colors map to
SGR 90 through 97 color values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Xterm palette bright colors (SGR 90-97 foreground, 100-107 background) map to: bright_black=#4C4C4C, bright_red=#FF0000, bright_green=#00FF00, bright_yellow=#FFFF00, bright_blue=#4682B4 (SteelBlue, not #0000FF), bright_magenta=#FF00FF, bright_cyan=#00FFFF, bright_white=#FFFFFF. Note bright_blue is #4682B4 not a pure blue. The vga palette uses different values: bright_black=#555555, bright_red=#FF5555, bright_green=#55FF55, bright_yellow=#FFFF55, bright_blue=#5555FF, bright_magenta=#FF55FF, bright_cyan=#55FFFF, bright_white=#FFFFFF.

trigger_keywords: 38;5 256 color indexed rendering
48;2 RGB true color background
how are extended color sequences parsed
256-color and truecolor escape format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Extended color sequences use the formats ESC[38;5;<n>m (256-color foreground), ESC[48;5;<n>m (256-color background), ESC[38;2;<r>;<g>;<b>m (truecolor foreground), ESC[48;2;<r>;<g>;<b>m (truecolor background). 256-color indexes 0-15 map to the standard palette colors, indexes 16-231 are a 6x6x6 color cube, and indexes 232-255 are a grayscale ramp. Truecolor values render directly as hex '#RRGGBB' regardless of palette. These extended colors are palette-independent for indexes 16-255 and truecolor.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter routes by the top-level operation field. Valid operation values are render_html, resolve_color_map, palette_summary, and insert_action_marker. The action field appears only inside insert_action_marker cases, where it is the marker value start or stop, not the dispatcher command.

trigger_keywords: ansicolor operation dispatcher
render_html resolve_color_map palette_summary insert_action_marker
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Route by operation. Valid operations: render_html, resolve_color_map, palette_summary, insert_action_marker. For insert_action_marker, the nested action value is start or stop and must not be treated as the top-level dispatcher command.

execution adapter

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled that marker injection thing in the annotation layer before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ConsoleNote passthrough logic in feature 1: concealed text segments whose payload starts with 'ha:' must be re-emitted verbatim with their ESC[8m...ESC[0m wrappers intact, as defined in feature1_plain_reset_and_conceal.json and the insert_action_marker operation in feature8_action_marker_rendering.json

The 'vga' palette has non-null default_foreground (index 7, #AAAAAA) and default_background (index 0, #000000) entries, causing render_html to wrap output in a '
...
' element, as shown in feature5_default_wrapping_and_unicode.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the action marker insertion feature also needs to spit out a specific output format with a normalized placeholder for the action id
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The insert_action_marker operation outputs 'annotated_text=...\n' with the marker '
' where '' is the literal placeholder string, not the actual action name — as defined in feature8_action_marker_rendering.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: we got burned last time by having everything tangled together
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture constraint from start.md: the core rendering logic (ANSI parser, style state machine, HTML emitter, palette registry) must be strictly separated from the I/O adapter layer (JSON stdin/stdout parsing), following SRP and DIP so that render_html and other domain methods can be called independently without JSON

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough technical context to answer precisely. You should look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — each feature has a JSON file with concrete input/output examples that should clarify the expected behavior. If it's about how a specific escape sequence should render, the test cases for that feature are the ground truth.

feature1_plain_reset_and_conceal.json.description: Render ordinary text, reset-only input, and concealed segments so that visible text is preserved while hidden ANSI text is omitted unless it is an embedded console note marker.

input.text: there is concealed text here, [8mCONCEAL[0m, and it should vanish.

cases.expected_output: there is concealed text here, , and it should vanish.

input.text: there is a ConsoleNote here, [8mha:CONSOLENOTE[0m, and it should be left untouched.

cases.expected_output: there is a ConsoleNote here, [8mha:CONSOLENOTE[0m, and it should be left untouched.

feature2_text_attributes.json.description: Render ANSI text-style attributes into nested HTML markup and close each style when its matching reset code is received.

cases.expected_output: lightblue and also faint

feature3_palette_colors.json.description: Render foreground and background color escape codes using the selected palette, including named terminal palettes, bright colors, indexed 256-color values, and RGB colors.

feature4_resets_nesting_and_inverse.json.description: Preserve style nesting across overlapping ANSI attributes, reset foreground and background independently, and invert foreground/background colors when inverse video is active.

cases.expected_output: onoff

feature5_default_wrapping_and_unicode.js.description: When a palette defines default foreground and background colors, wrap rendered output in a container using those defaults and preserve non-ASCII text while applying ANSI styling.

feature6_escape_filtering_and_real_logs..description: Remove cursor/control escape sequences and redundant reset escapes from log-like input while preserving visible log text and valid color markup.
